chunk_summary: Outpatient management of pediatric anemia involves phenotype assessment, oral iron trials, response monitoring, and adherence to clinical guidelines for dosage and follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic approach for pediatric iron deficiency anemia in outpatient settings.
Learn the recommended oral iron dosages and response monitoring protocols.
Identify nonresponse triggers and alternative evaluation pathways for anemia management.
key_facts: Iron deficiency anemia is suggested by pallor, microcytosis, high RDW, and poor dietary iron intake.
Therapeutic oral iron dose: 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron; lower doses (2–3 mg/kg/day) may improve adherence.
Hemoglobin rise of ≥1 g/dL after 2 weeks indicates iron responsiveness.
Treatment duration: 8–12 weeks to normalize hemoglobin and replenish iron stores.
Ferritin levels should be interpreted alongside CRP due to inflammation-related elevation.
reference_quote: The therapeutic oral dose is usually 3–6 mg/kg/day of elemental iron; many children respond to 2–3 mg/kg/day with fewer gastrointestinal adverse effects, improving adherence. (IAP Standard Treatment Guidelines)
document_key_topics: Oral iron therapy and response monitoring
Clinical management of pediatric iron deficiency anemia
